basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: The Fātiḥa — "The Opening" — is the mother of the Qurʾān (umm al-Qurʾān), the seven oft-repeated verses recited in every prayer. A Meccan sūra of seven verses, it holds in miniature all that the Book unfolds: praise, the Names of mercy, the Lordship of the worlds, the Sovereignty of the Day of Judgment, exclusive worship and help-seeking, the request for the straight path. Qushayrī makes it the mystical threshold par excellence: the basmala opens the Book so that the mention of "Allāh" may descend upon a heart cleansed of its attachments and a secret freed of its hindrances. From the "Him" of majesty ("Lord of the worlds") to the "You" of intimacy ("You alone we worship"), the sūra accomplishes the passage from narration to address, from the contemplation of the attributes to the face-to-face of love. Worship here unveils its secret — its outward is lowliness, its truth is elevation — and the guidance requested is not mere moral orientation but a burning supplication: be Yourself our guide to You, and bring us across from the stations of seeking to the open spaces of union. It is sealed with "Āmīn," the poor one's clinging to the permanence of help.
themes: the Names of mercy, universal and elective
the Sovereignty of the Day of Judgment
worship and help-seeking referred to God alone
the straight path and the request for constancy
those graced with favor, those who incur wrath, and those who go astray

versets.trad: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
versets.commentaire: The basmala opens the Qurʾān and, in the Fātiḥa, forms its first verse. Qushayrī dwells at length on the opening bāʾ of "bismi-Llāh": through God all things came to be, through Him the creatures received existence; there is no being, no trace, no ruin — stone or dust, star or tree — that does not hold its existence from the Real: from the Real is its beginning, and to the Real its return. He explains why one says "in the name of God" rather than "by God": to seek the blessing of His Name, and so that hearts may be cleansed of their attachments and secrets freed of their hindrances, that the mention of "Allāh" may descend upon a purified heart and a clarified secret. Some, hearing the bāʾ, recall His goodness (birr) toward His saints; the sīn, His secret (sirr) among His elect; the mīm, His favor (minna) upon His own.

mots.def: the bāʾ marks instrument and dependence: all is through Him.
Allāh: the proper Name of the Godhead, His alone.
al-Raḥmān: intensive form of mercy, a Name exclusive to God, universal in scope.
al-Raḥīm: mercy as it acts upon creation, particular to the believers.

versets.trad: Praise be to God, Lord of the worlds,
versets.commentaire: "Praise be to God": the all-encompassing lām gathers every praise back to God alone, whether attribute or creature. Qushayrī ranks those who praise: some praise Him for the favors received, others for what has shone in their hearts of the wonders of His subtleties, and others still praise Him while contemplating the attributes of Eternity, without turning back from the vision of His munificence to the reckoning of His gifts. And he marvels that God praises Himself in order to raise His servants: knowing the ardor of His saints to glorify Him and their inability to fulfill His praise as His majesty deserves, He informed them that He has praised Himself — so they revived after extinction and rose up after abasement. As for "Lord of the worlds" (rabb al-ʿālamīn): the Lord is Master and Educator — He nurtures the souls of the worshippers through support, the hearts of the seekers through rectitude, the spirits of the knowers through union.

mots.def: ḥamd: praise of the Praised for His sublime attributes and beautiful acts.
the lām assigns all praise to God alone.
rabb: master, owner, and educator of every being.
al-ʿālamūn: the whole of created beings, rational and inert.

versets.commentaire: The two Names return, both drawn from the root raḥma — an eternal attribute, which is the will to bestow blessing. Qushayrī distinguishes them finely: al-Raḥmān is a Name particular in form, universal in meaning; al-Raḥīm a Name universal in form, particular in meaning. By His being Raḥmān He provides every creature with what eases its outward life; by His being Raḥīm He grants the believers what gives life to their inmost secret: al-Raḥmān through the unveiling of His self-disclosure, al-Raḥīm through the gentleness of His care; al-Raḥmān through the faith He bestows, al-Raḥīm through the gnosis He dispenses. Mercy thus precedes all: by it the servant came to know that his Lord is Raḥmān, and without it none would have known Him.

mots.def: overflowing mercy embracing all creation.
elective mercy that vivifies the hearts of the believers.

versets.trad: Sovereign of the Day of Judgment.
versets.commentaire: "Master of the Day of Recompense" (mālik yawm al-dīn). The Master is He who holds dominion; the Real's dominion is His power to bring existence into being: as none is God but He, so none can create but He; unique in His divinity, alone in His sovereignty. Qushayrī unfolds this sovereignty into the hearts of the saints: He has possessed the souls of the worshippers and turned them to His service, possessed the hearts of the knowers and ennobled them with His knowledge; He has made the people of Unity know that He is their Master, and their own choice fell away — they knew that the servant possesses nothing, and one who possesses nothing has neither verdict nor choice. And "the Day of Recompense" (yawm al-dīn) is the Day of reckoning and gathering: He requites each as He wills; some are received by His grace, not by their works.

mots.def: mālik: the one who holds dominion and free disposal.
yawm: the day, the moment of the final event.
al-dīn: judgment, the requital of deeds.

versets.trad: You alone we worship, and from You alone we seek help.
versets.commentaire: The verse shifts from "Him" to "You": from the account of majesty to direct address. Qushayrī: to begin by mentioning the Worshipped is more perfect than to begin by mentioning His attribute — which is our worship and our seeking of help. He contrasts the two movements: worship speaks of the expending of effort, help-seeking announces the drawing of grace; through worship the servant's nobility appears, through help-seeking the gentleness of God comes to him. And he unveils the secret of worship: its outward is lowliness, its truth is elevation and honor — when the necks bow toward You seeking nearness, their glory lies in their humility. Worship, he adds, is the pleasance of the seekers, the rest of the aspirants, the garden of intimacy for the lovers, the meadow of joy for the knowers.

mots.def: fronted pronoun of exclusivity: it is You, to the exclusion of all else.
ʿibāda: worship, the utmost submission, in conformity with the command.
emphatic repetition of the exclusivity.
istiʿāna: the request for aid addressed to the Real alone.

versets.trad: Guide us upon the straight path,
versets.commentaire: "Guide us upon the straight path." Guidance (hidāya) is orientation; yet the believers are already guided — so the meaning of the request is permanence and increase. Qushayrī turns "guide us" into a burning prayer: incline us toward You, take us for Yourself, be Yourself our guide to You, smooth for us the way to You, raise up our high resolves and gather our cares into You. Then he interiorizes it: sever our secrets from the vision of "others," let the dawns of the lights break in our hearts, and bring us across from the stations of seeking and reasoning to the open spaces of nearness and union. The straight path, he says, is the one to which the proof of Unity bears witness, the one the Book and the Sunna point out, over which innovation has neither power nor access.

mots.def: hadā: to guide, to incline toward; a request for constancy in guidance.
ṣirāṭ: the broad, well-trodden road.
mustaqīm: straight, without deviation — the way of the people of Unity.

versets.ar: صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا الضَّالِّينَ
versets.tr: ṣirāṭa lladhīna anʿamta ʿalayhim ghayri l-maghḍūbi ʿalayhim wa-lā ḍ-ḍāllīna
versets.trad: the path of those You have blessed, not of those who incur Your wrath, nor of those who go astray.
versets.commentaire: "The path of those You have blessed": the way of those You have favored with guidance to the straight path — the saints and the elect. Qushayrī hears it higher still: the way of those You made extinct to themselves and established by You, for You, so that they did not halt along the road and the hidden snares did not turn them from You. As for "those who incur wrath" (al-maghḍūb ʿalayhim): those struck by the onslaughts of abandonment, overtaken by the calamities of deprivation, overpowered by the blows of rejection; and "those who go astray" (al-ḍāllūn) were broken in the deserts of absence, scattered by their cares through the valleys of conjecture. At the close the servant says "Āmīn": O Lord, do and answer — and the strongest intercession of the poor one is his clinging to the permanence of seeking help.

mots.racine: ص ر ط / ن ع م
mots.sens: the path of those You have blessed
mots.def: anʿama: to bless, to grace; these are the prophets, saints, and elect.

mots.sens: not of those who incur wrath
mots.def: al-maghḍūb ʿalayhim: those who have drawn down the divine anger.

mots.sens: nor of those who go astray
mots.def: al-ḍāllūn: those who have lost the way, wandering in conjecture.
